old_claim.note: Hospital Information Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains from dialysis_machines to hospital_information_systems; dialysis machine architecture and early chronology depend on membranes, fluid flow, and clinical treatment, not hospital IT.

source_shape.source_locator: Kolff and Scribner account of artificial kidney construction and clinical dialysis history.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes hemodialysis through cellophane tubing, dialyzing fluid, artificial kidneys, patient treatments, and later access systems without hospital information systems.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This refutes the HIS edge because the cited device history predates modern hospital IT and explains the working technology through physical dialysis components and clinical care.

ontology_before: Dialysis machines depended on hospital information systems, mixing a treatment device with later administration and record infrastructure.
ontology_after: Dialysis machines are modeled through membrane separation, pump/flow scaling, and clinical treatment context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: hospital information systems no longer appear as a direct dialysis-machine prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to modern dialysis-clinic information platforms rather than dialysis machines.
A source showed the dialysis machine itself requires hospital information-system infrastructure.
Removing the HIS edge also removed clinical medicine from the node.
short_reason: Hospital information systems are care-administration infrastructure, not a dialysis-machine prerequisite.
